items.question_text: Under 9-8-1, who must be informed when UAP or unexplained phenomena activity is reported or observed?
items.explanation: Per 9-8-1, inform the operations supervisor/CIC of any reported or observed UAP or unexplained phenomena activity.

choices.text: All aircraft on frequency via broadcast advisory.
The nearest military facility under a standing Letter of Agreement.
The operations supervisor/CIC.
The FAA Regional Office only, after the shift ends.

items.question_text: Under 9-8-1, what two categories of phenomena trigger the reporting obligation?
items.explanation: Per 9-8-1, the obligation applies to unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP) AND unexplained phenomena activity. Both reported and observed instances are covered.

choices.text: Only phenomena confirmed by at least two independent pilot reports.
Unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP) and unexplained phenomena activity.
Only phenomena that appear on primary radar without a corresponding transponder return.
Only airborne phenomena; ground-based observations are excluded.

items.question_text: Under 9-8-1, how must the UAP activity be known to the controller to trigger the reporting obligation?
items.explanation: Per 9-8-1, the obligation is triggered by any reported OR observed UAP/unexplained phenomena activity. The controller does not need to personally observe it — a report from a pilot or another source is sufficient.

choices.text: It must be personally observed by the controller on radar or visually.
It must be either reported (by pilots or others) or observed (by the controller or facility). Either source triggers the obligation.
It must be confirmed by both a pilot report AND radar observation.
It must be reported by at least three independent sources to be actionable.

items.question_text: Under 9-8-1, a pilot reports unusual lights that the controller cannot see on radar. Is the controller obligated to inform the supervisor?
items.explanation: Yes. 9-8-1 applies to any reported OR observed activity. A pilot report triggers the obligation regardless of whether the controller can independently verify it on radar.

choices.text: No, because the controller must have independent radar confirmation before reporting.
No, because UAP reports only apply to objects that are visually confirmed by ATC personnel.
Yes, but only if the pilot declares an emergency in connection with the sighting.
Yes, because 9-8-1 applies to any reported UAP or unexplained phenomena activity.

items.question_text: Under 9-8-1, what FAA Order provides additional procedures for UAP reports?
items.explanation: The reference cites FAA Order JO 7210.3, Paragraph 4-7-4, Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP) Reports for additional procedures.

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 10.
14 CFR Part 101.
FAA Order JO 7210.3, Paragraph 4-7-4.
FAA Order JO 7210.3, Paragraph 19-5-2.

items.explanation: UAP stands for Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena, as defined in the paragraph text.

choices.text: Unidentified Aircraft Procedure.
Unknown Airborne Phenomenon.
Unidentified Aerial Phenomena.
Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena.

items.situation: A pilot reports fast-moving lights at an unusual altitude with no corresponding radar return. A trainee says to log it and mention it at the end of the shift because it doesn't affect separation and may just be a weather balloon.
items.question_text: What does 9-8-1 require in this situation?

choices.text: Inform the operations supervisor/CIC of the reported UAP/unexplained phenomena activity immediately.
Wait until the end of the shift to file a report, because no separation was affected.
Ignore the report unless the pilot can provide photographic evidence of the sighting.
Broadcast an advisory to all aircraft on the frequency before informing supervision.

items.explanation: Per 9-8-1, any reported or observed UAP or unexplained phenomena activity must be reported to the operations supervisor/CIC. There is no requirement to wait until end of shift or for independent confirmation.
